does: This paper introduces Align-then-Slide, a comprehensive evaluation framework designed to assess machine translation of ultra-long documents produced by large language models. The framework operates in two stages: first, it automatically aligns source and target sentences to resolve structural mismatches like omissions and many-to-one mappings, then rebuilds the target to match source sentence counts. Second, it evaluates translations at multiple granularities using a sliding window approach across 1-, 2-, 3-, and 4-chunk segments, computing averaged metric scores. The method achieves a Pearson correlation of 0.929 with expert MQM rankings on WMT benchmarks and demonstrates strong alignment with human judgments on a real-world test set. Additionally, preference data generated by the framework effectively trains models via contrastive preference optimization and reward modeling, producing translations superior to standard supervised fine-tuning baselines.
connects: This work addresses a fundamental gap exposed by the shift toward document-level machine translation with LLMs. Traditional sentence-level evaluation metrics assume one-to-one source-target sentence correspondence, an assumption violated when LLMs process and translate entire documents holistically. The paper builds on decades of MT evaluation research, from metrics like BLEU and METEOR to learned metrics and human evaluation frameworks like MQM, extending these approaches to document-scale outputs with structural misalignment. It also connects to recent work on LLM-based translation and preference-based model training, where reliable evaluation signals are critical for optimization. The alignment problem itself draws from sequence-to-sequence correspondence techniques and document understanding literature, adapting these methods to handle the idiosyncratic output structures that emerge from generative translation models.
enables: This framework enables more rigorous evaluation and optimization of document-level machine translation systems, opening pathways for future research. By producing reliable preference signals, it facilitates preference-based training methods like GRPO and CPO, allowing the community to move beyond single-reference evaluation toward human-aligned reward models for translation. The multi-granularity assessment approach suggests directions for studying how translation quality varies across document scales and context windows. The framework could extend to other language generation tasks with document-level outputs, including summarization and question-answering. Furthermore, the automatic alignment methodology might inform better handling of structural divergences in other multilingual NLP tasks. The connection to reward modeling for translation opens opportunities to integrate aligned evaluation frameworks into reinforcement learning pipelines for pursuing human-preferred rather than metric-optimized translations at scale.
